results.title: A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
results.content:
AgenticSTS logo

# AgenticSTS

A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents

Memory for a long-horizon agent is a contract about what each future decision is allowed to see. AgenticSTS makes that contract bounded, typed, and ablatable — and releases a reproducible Silent A0 benchmark in Slay the Spire 2 where this design wins games that tested public transcript agents did not.

📄 Paper (arXiv) 🤗 Paper (HF) 💻 Code 🤗 Dataset 📦 Frozen stores

bounded prompt · independent of run length 5 typed layers · each ablatable 298 released trajectories model-agnostic · Gemini · GPT · Qwen · Claude

AgenticSTS overview: the growing-transcript problem, the bounded memory contract, why Slay the Spire 2 is a testbed, and what the release enables [...] The problem (unbounded transcript memory), the bounded contract, why Slay the Spire 2 is a long-horizon testbed, and what the release provides.

Unedited gameplay

## Watch it play

The agent driving real runs — combat, elites and bosses, map routing, shops, events, rest sites, card rewards, and run outcomes.

Abstract

## The opposite of “append everything”

Most long-horizon LLM agents use the simplest memory contract: append everything — past observations, tool calls, reflections — to every prompt. Context grows without bound, stale traces re-enter decisions, and when the agent improves, no one can say which memory component did it. [...] ```
@article{agenticsts2026, title = {AgenticSTS: A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents}, author = {Cheng, Xiangchen and Jiang, Yunwei and Sun, Jianwen and Li, Zizhen and Li, Chuanhao and Cao, Xiangcheng and Liu, Yihao and Zhang, Fanrui and Jin, Li and Zhang, Kaipeng}, year = {2026}, eprint = {2607.02255}, archivePrefix = {arXiv}, url = { }
```

results.title: AgenticSTS Testbed Doubles LLM Wins in Slay the Spire 2 | AI Weekly
results.content:
Read the original article → 

Original headline: HF Paper 'AgenticSTS': Bounded-Memory Testbed Uses Slay the Spire 2 to Study Long-Horizon LLM Agents — Adding Strategic Skills Layer Doubles Wins From 3/10 to 6/10 Where Frontier LLMs Previously Reported Zero

Free AI alerts in your inbox Breaking AI news 3x/week. 44,000+ subscribers.

We use essential cookies to keep the site working (login, form security). With your permission, we also use analytics cookies to understand how you use the site. Privacy policy [...] Most agent research right now is a race to stuff more tokens into context and hope the model sorts it out. The AgenticSTS paper on Hugging Face goes the other way. Every decision is made from a fresh user message assembled by typed retrieval, no raw cross-decision transcript is appended, and prompts stay bounded across runs of any length. The point of the design is that individual memory layers can be ablated in isolation, which is the thing that has been missing from most long-horizon agent write-ups.

results.title: Why Your AI Agent Keeps Failing: The Memory Problem No One Talks About | by Rizwanhoda | Jun, 2026 | Towards AI
results.content:
I spent a frustrated weekend thinking the agent’s reasoning was broken. It wasn’t. The reasoning was fine. The problem was that my agent had no real memory architecture just a conversation history getting longer and longer, with no structure, no prioritization, and no way to distinguish “important fact from step 2” from “irrelevant tool…

results.title: Bounded memory tool with hard character limits · Issue #42877 · openclaw/openclaw · GitHub
results.content:
## Description

@iFiras-Max1

Hey,

I've been running OpenClaw as my daily driver for about five weeks now with a fairly heavy setup (16 crons, multi-agent delegation, Telegram gateway). One thing I keep running into is memory bloat.

The problem

Workspace memory files (MEMORY.md, etc.) grow without any guardrails. My MEMORY.md is at ~3,800 bytes and climbing. The agent keeps appending but rarely consolidates or removes stale entries. Over time:

`memory_search`

I've tried adding prompt-level rules ("keep memory under X chars"), but they get ignored under load or after compaction. There's no hard enforcement. [...] ## Navigation Menu

# Bounded memory tool with hard character limits #42877

## Description

@iFiras-Max1

Hey, [...] Related: #26949 raises the same underlying issue, MEMORY.md is fully injected despite memory\_search/memory\_get being available. A bounded write tool would complement that discussion nicely.

What I'm proposing

A built-in `memory` tool that manages bounded memory stores with hard character limits:

`memory`
`memory(action: "add", target: "memory", content: "Project uses Axum + SQLx, tests via cargo test")
memory(action: "replace", target: "memory", old_text: "uses Axum", content: "Migrated to Actix-Web")
memory(action: "remove", target: "memory", old_text: "Migrated to Actix")`

Key behaviors:

Why a tool and not a skill?
